courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Drawing Fundamentals
courses.description: This course provides students with basic knowledge and skills in drawing. Topics include: (1) tools and techniques; (2) perspective and observation; (3) composition and visual element; and (4) perception and expression.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- master the fundamental techniques of drawing and mode of representation; and

- develop own forms of expression

Observation, Reaction and Representation: Still-life Drawing in Pencil

Observation, Reaction and Representation: Plaster Cast Figure Drawing in Charcoal

Observation, Reaction and Representation: Photo-real Drawing in Pencil

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Principles of Chinese Calligraphy
courses.description: This course is offered for beginners in Chinese calligraphy. It describes the basic concepts and techniques of Chinese calligraphy, the selection and use of materials and tools, and teaches students the regular, clerical and seal scripts. Students will be prepared for more advanced courses of various scripts.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- manage general knowledge and usage of the four treasures of Chinese studio;
- recognize the characteristics of different formats of Chinese calligraphy; and

- apply what they learn in the class to the creation of elementary Chinese calligraphy in clerical and regular scripts
courses.course_syllabus:
Tang Dynasty regular script, “Ni Kuan Zan倪寬贊” by Chu Suiliang（褚遂良）

Eastern Han Dynasty inscribed stele, “Stele of Ritual Vessels (Li Qi Bei 禮器碑)”

the lesser seal script by the Qing Dynasty calligrapher, Deng Shiru（鄧石如）

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Principles of Chinese Painting
courses.description: This course introduces the materials and tools, techniques in brushwork, ink tone variations, and colouring to beginning students of Chinese painting. Related skills such as mounting, writing of inscriptions, imprinting of seals will also be explored. Students will practise traditional paintings of pine trees, rocks, bamboo, and human figures, etc., in preparation for in-depth studies in landscape, figure or bird-and-flower paintings.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- understand the content and basic concept of Chinese painting;
- manage basic techniques in brushwork, tone variation and coloring of Chinese painting; and

- apply what they learn in the class to the creation of elementary Chinese painting
courses.feedback_evaluation: Evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. The CU Forum will also be used if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Fundamentals of 3-Dimensional Forming
courses.description: This course provides students with basic concepts and skills in three-dimensional forming through various material processes, both in traditional handicraft and digital 3D forming environment. Emphasis will be placed on three major aspects: 1) the perception of form and space; 2) materials and construction; and 3) 3D form as artistic expression. The course will also help students to get familiar with studio operations that would equip them for further sculptural practice.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Observing Form & Space

Understanding the nature as well as the structure of diverse natural and man-made forms and spaces.

2. Transformation of Form & Space

Explore various ways of form-space-transformation that lead to ideas/concepts of 3D artistic expression.

3. From 2D Plane to 3D Form

Pattern cutting as a means of analyzing, de-constructing and re-constructing a 3D form.

4. 3D Construction - from Handicraft to Digital Forming and vice-versa

Investigate wide range of possibilities to further develop a hand-built sculptural form within a digital environment. Usage of 3D scanner and printer will be introduced and practiced. The final piece should be a combination of hand-crafted and digital forming.

5. The Container & the Contained

A tailor-made container / box will be constructed to house the sculptural form developed in the previous stages. Relevant images as well as different materials / textures will be explored and eventually applied onto the container / box to further reference the conceptual development of the sculptural form. Usage of appropriate tools and machinery will be introduced and practiced.
courses.feedback_evaluation: A clear teaching plan will be provided before the course commences. An evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. An on line discussion platform may be installed if appropriate.

courses.description:
(UGEA1310 is double-coded with FAAS1600.)

In light of artifacts whether excavated or collected as well as historical texts, this course provides an overview of Chinese art over 5000 years. Through studying the art and culture of the pre-historic era and the Bronze Age, and the development of painting, calligraphy, religious sculpture, architecture, jade, gold, silver, and ceramics throughout the centuries, students will understand the special characteristics and historical development of Chinese art and culture over the centuries. Artistic exchange between China and its neighbouring countries will be given special attention. Tutorial sessions involve practical workshop or the study of actual art objects at the Art Museum.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Artistic Traditions in the West
courses.description: The goal of this course is to investigate Western art practices from ancient times to the modern era. Analyses of key works of art will be considered in relationship to the social, political, economic and cultural circumstances that surrounded and informed them.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- describe general knowledge and various period of development of Western Arts from Greek art to the present period; and

- appreciate artworks according to formal criteria: for example, analyze an artwork using more advanced formal analysis.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Introduction to Art History
courses.description: This course aims to develop students’ abilities to “read” artwork. Students will gain a basic understanding that besides aesthetics and forms, artwork represents the culture, philosophy, religion, technology, literature, and history when it was produced and collected. Critical art historical concepts including but limited to style, mass production, iconography, patronage, narrative, connoisseurship, and forgery, will be used to analyze masterpieces to hone students’ object-based research skills.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. How to look at a work of art

2. How to present a work of art

3. How to write an art historical essay

4. Style

5. Mass production

6. Iconography

7. Patronage

8. Narrative

9. Fine art and craft

10. Connoisseurship

11. Forgery

12. Literati art
courses.feedback_evaluation: A clear teaching plan will be provided before the course commences. An evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Reading Chinese Painting
courses.description:
(UGED2338 is double-coded with FAAS2101.)
Symbols and images that carry traces of Chinese cultural traditions can be found everywhere in the modern world. This course provides a comprehensive overview of the history of Chinese painting and trains students’ abilities in reading images so that they can achieve a deeper understanding of the cultural meanings embedded in everyday encounters. Through a broad survey of Chinese painting from the pre-Song period to the present, students will become familiar with major works, figures, theories, and movements, while also developing fundamental art-historical skills for observing and analyzing images’ style, subject matter, and socio-cultural meaning. In-class discussion of readings and writing exercises will teach students how to read scholarly literature and translate their observations and research on paintings into various forms of writing, such as academic reports, museum labels, or catalogue entries.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to the course: Ice breaker

This introductory session will guide students through reading selected entries from Maxwell K. Hearn, *How to Read Chinese* *Paintings* (The Metropolitan Museum of Art and Yale University Press, 2008), to help them understand the basic formats and relationship between imagery and text in Chinese painting.

2. Figural painting (I): Line

This session will introduce major figural paintings from the pre-Song period and examine the historical, cultural, and literary contexts in which they emerged. It also explores the significance of “line” as a fundamental expressive element in Chinese painting.

3. Figural painting (II): Narrative

This session will investigate major figural paintings from the Song period onward and analyze the historical and cultural factors that led to the gradual decline of figural painting as a dominant genre in later periods.

4. Landscape painting (I): Style

This session will analyze the stylistic characteristics of landscape paintings from northern and southern China prior to the Southern Song period and examine the political and philosophical contexts that contributed to the emergence of this new genre as a representative form of Chinese painting.

5. Landscape painting (II): Text and image

This session will explore the relationship between text/poem and image in paintings from the Southern Song period onward, as well as the related yet distinct aesthetic ideals associated with courtly, Buddhist, and literati contexts.

6. Landscape painting (III): Painting schools

This session will examine the specific temporal and spatial conditions, political environment, and social structures that contributed to the emergence of Chinese “painting schools” in the Ming and Qing periods.

7. Paintings genres and art collecting

This session will examine the importance of art collecting within the Chinese political tradition and survey a range of lesser-known genres of Chinese painting, considering their changing significance within major art collections from the Song period to the present.

8. Bird-and-flower painting: Ink and color

This session will examine major bird-and-flower paintings, analyzing their artistic techniques as well as their cultural, political, and literary significance across historical periods.

9. Religious painting (I): Iconography

This session will introduce the transmission, visual features, and theological aspects of Chinese Buddhist and Daoist painting, with particular emphasis on the methods and significance of iconographical research.

10. Religious painting (II): Painting workshop

This session will explore the workshop practices involved in the creation of Chinese paintings, as well as the political and economic contexts in which they emerged.

2. Internal Course Review

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Introduction to Asian Art: The Art and Archaeology of West, South, Southeast, and Inner Asia
courses.description:
(UGED2315 is double-coded with FAAS2113.)

The survey introduces the art and material culture of pre-20th century Asia with particular attention paid to trans-regional and cross-cultural interactions. Select artistic traditions in West (mainly, Iran), South (India, Sri Lanka, and the Himalayas), Southeast (Indonesia, Burma, Thai, Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam), and Inner (Afghanistan, Tajikistan, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Turkmenistan, and Mongolia) will be examined against their philosophical, religious, socio-political, economic, aesthetic, and technological backgrounds. Major themes include (1) the rise of civilizations, (2) developments of various artistic forms and media, (3) tombs and funerary paraphernalia, (4) the spread of Buddhism, Hinduism, Islamism, and other worldviews across the region, and (5) Indianization and Sinicization.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Western Art I: from the 15th to the Late 19th Century
courses.description: This course aims to give a broad overview of Western art from the 15th to the late 19th century. The Renasissance is universally regarded as a fundamental period in the history of Western Art. Subsequent period styles, such as Mannerism, Baroque, Neo-Classicism, Romanticism and Realism are either development from the art principles of the Renaissance or reactions against the rules and bounds developed during this important period. Special attention will be given to the major artworks of each movement as well as the historical events which have influenced the evolution of Western art.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- know the general timeframe of the developments of Western art from the early Renaissance to the late 19th century;
- situate an artwork (sculpture, painting and architecture) within a time period: for example, recognize whether a painting is a High Renaissance or a work of the Romantic period; and

- understand the historical background of each period.
courses.course_syllabus:
- Late Gothic and Early Renaissance in Northern Europe

- Early Renaissance in Italy and the rest of Europe

- High Renaissance and Mannerism

- Baroque: Caravaggism in Europe

- Baroque and Classicism

- Continental Rococo and English art

- Neoclassicism

- Romanticism and its period

- Realism and its period

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Mineral-pigment Painting
courses.description:
In the history of Chinese art, particularly in the realm of Chinese mural art, the use of mineral pigments as the primary painting medium is now known as mineral-pigment painting. Mineral-pigment painting once had a glorious past, and the Dunhuang murals are still hailed as classic works of art.
This course hopes to inherit and protect the precious cultural heritage of mineral-pigment painting, and to expand the use of the aesthetic qualities of mineral pigments, combined with individual creative concepts and expression techniques, to become a unique modern painting medium.
As an ancient painting material, mineral pigment has both the heavy-body covering characteristics of oil and the transparency of watercolor, and now is being ‘ rediscovered’ by many contemporary artists ; not only the course will cover traditional method, but will also explore various possibilities in contemporary expression of mineral pigment painting.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Classical Chinese Mural Copying:**

Through the copying of ancient Chinese murals, students will understand the history of murals and master the materials and techniques used in traditional mural painting.

* *2. Sketching and Creation:**

Cultivate students' interest and ability to translate observable world into visual language of mineral-pigment , and then use their imagination to create personal styles and convey their own ideas.

* *3. Color Composition and Configuration:**

Mineral pigments are insoluble in any solvent and cannot be mixed together; therefore, color expression is achieved through the use of configuration techniques and the composition of color layers.

* *4. Lecture on the Production Steps of Mineral-Pigment Painting Supports:**

Understand and master the preparation of glue, alum paper, mounting boards, texture creation, and the making of clam powder grounds.

* *5. Understanding the Types, Characteristics, and Techniques of Mineral-Pigment Painting Colors:**

Mineral pigments, earth pigments, enamel colors, and plant watercolors, etc.

* *6. Metal Foil Application Techniques:**

Applying, burning, cutting, and sprinkling foil, etc.

* *7. Practical Steps of Mineral-Pigment Painting .**

* *8. Analysis and Appreciation of Classic Works of Mineral-Pigment Painting from Ancient and Modern Times, both Domestic and International.**

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Becoming a Comics Director
courses.description:
In addition to artistic skills such as drawing, composition and the understanding of color, storytelling is one of the essentials of comics art. These skills in isolation are however not sufficient to create original comics and this art form can only exist through the proper arrangement of visual elements and image-text interactions. This course aims to provide students with the necessary understanding to further develop their understanding of visual and narrative communication through the creation of a story in the form of comics. In order to enhance students’ capacity to create such an interaction of narrative and images, workshops in the assisted creation of stories will be provided alongside the development of the student’s own visual research. Students will also be encouraged to find their own visual language and graphic style.
Comic writing is a demanding profession that requires a number of specialized skills. This course will offer training and tutorials in the skills most appropriate to comics creation such as character design, script writing and the kind of sensitive observation that leads to intelligent creativity. Other specialized skills, such as insights into the marketing of comics and management of high-pressure schedules, will also be presented to students; this course will prepare potential comics creators for any future challenge in the profession.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: FAAS1100
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

1. Understand and master comics drawing tools as well as basic digital technical skills

2. Research a comics project through the creation of a sketch book

3. Understand and master character design

4. Understand and master story telling techniques for comics

5. Understand and master the creation of a story board

6. Present their own projects in the form of presentation and the creation of a comics fans page

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Mixed Media
courses.description: After World War II, art media are no longer confined to specific materials, tools and techniques. Artists tend to think and make art through idea rather than material. This has caused confusion between conceptual art and visual art. This course will teach students the basic concepts of mixed media, and provide students with a framework for artistic creation, when no well-defined boundaries for material and technique can serve as a reference. Focus will be put on the three principles of mixed media, namely, physicality, cultural signification and spirituality.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to understand:

- how to practice contemporary art, such as concept, method and it’s operation in society

- how concept could be elaborated and manifested through material

- how to appreciate idea driven art

- develop individual skill and approach of mixed media; and

- critical thinking in art
courses.course_syllabus:
- The three principal of Mixed Media, namely, Physicality, Cultural Significance and Spirituality.

- Art and society—Relational/ Interventional approach of art

- Develop and adapt individual presentation skill for various ideas

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Art, Archive and Technology
courses.description:
This course examines the intersection of contemporary art practices with archives, data analytics, and technology. It delves into the dialectic relations between history, archive, and art. Furthermore, we will explore immersive narratives as a means to showcase contemporary art practices, using archives to investigate the present and envision the future.
The course begins with an analysis of seminal theoretical texts and case studies in archival art practices. We will then delve into the fundamental concepts of data analytics, exploring how algorithms and big data have transformed our methods of acquiring knowledge. Students will critically assess the approaches and challenges of artists’ archival practices and data analytics tools through theoretical discussions and case studies of various artworks. These discussions will emphasize how artists engage with data or inventories to interpret and think. Students will complete a creative project to synthesize their learning.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Introduction: Archival Art Practice in Digital Age**

In this introductory session, we will cover fundamental ideas and debates surrounding archival art practice in contemporary art. How do the digital aspects that challenge traditional archival history reshape our understanding of archival art practice? To what extent can these challenges be leveraged as opportunities for art practice using digitally enhanced tools within Digital Humanities?

This class will also introduce the course structure, reading materials, teaching methods, timetable, and assignments.

* *2. The Archival Impulse and Contemporary Contingencies**

This class will explore the powerful impulse to archive in contemporary culture and its significance in historical knowledge and remembrance. It will also examine the evolving attitudes toward archives in modern and postmodern art, focusing on how artists engage with archival practices and create counter-archives.

* *3. Memory, Forgetting, and Re-presentation in Archival Art**

This class will investigate the role of memory and forgetting in archival art. It will discuss how traces in documents and archives serve as testimony to past events and how artists use these traces to create new narratives. The concept of “détournement” in collage and the practice of collecting will also be explored.

* *4. The Digital Archive and Ephemeral Art**

This class will examine the impact of digital technologies on archival practices, focusing on the transition from physical to digital archives. It will discuss the fluid and dynamic nature of digital archives and their potential for new memory cultures. The class will also explore the ephemeral nature of contemporary archival art and its implications for preservation and memory.

* *5 What is Data Science?**
This class will cover the fundamental concepts of data science and the latest developments in AI for text analysis, particularly in the context of big data. We will also consider the impact of data on archival practices. Additionally, we will discuss the features and limitations of data mining in cultural analytics.

* *6. Data Science Tools**

Explore the tools of data science that relate to archival art and the ethical considerations of using AI tools. Discuss the concept of algorithms in text analysis, focusing on the open-source data mining software, which uses a set of rules or procedures to perform various data analysis tasks. Examine the impact of digital technologies on text analysis and data visualization.

* *7. Workshops on Digital Preservation**

In this class, students will be introduced to the fundamental technologies of virtuality, under the category of Extended Reality (XR) and 3D scanning. We will discuss the unique characteristics of computational media, distinguishing it from other forms of media. And its application on digital preservation.

* *8. Workshops on Virtual Reality**

This workshop will teach students the basic use of software and hardware tools to create virtual immersive narratives. Students will gain practical skills for integrating technology into their artistic practices, enhancing their ability to produce digital art experiences with articulation of concepts and methods from the course.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Digital Imaging Workshop
courses.description: The course is designed to explore the concepts, aesthetics, and techniques of digital imaging from both academic and studio perspectives. Both technical and aesthetic issues will be addressed. Aesthetic issues encompass concept, composition and historical context, whereas technical topics include raster and vector imaging, image manipulation, time-based 2D graphics, animation and other related topics. Digital imaging software and hardware will also be introduced to illustrate the principles and techniques and to produce the projects.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- create still 2D compositions exploring visual elements such as points, lines, forms, colors, and symbols, in a digital environment;
- create 2D compositions and animations with digitally captured images, computer-generated images and found images with a sense of rhythm and structure; and

- present computer-based artwork (image) with appropriate presentation hardware and space, such as projection, screen, monitor, and printing materials.
courses.course_syllabus:
1 Digital imaging: Creating and capturing image digitally, as well as obtaining from other analog mediums

2 Motion graphics: Applying special effects, compositing, and color correction to collected and computer-generated images in order to explore transformation of basic visual elements

3 Animation: Making use of sequence of images in a video format for creating narrative

4Text and Image: Exploring visual communication through graphics, text and digital collage

5 Abstract Animation: Exploring visual rhythm with basic visual elements in animation format

6 Web Art: Understanding approaches of delivering aesthetic experiences via the Internet

courses.description: The aim of this course is to explore the technology of ancient Chinese ceramics through both lectures and workshops. A selection of classic wares from major kilns will be discussed in the lectures. Focuses will be placed on the technique of firing, forming and decorating ceramics and the related kiln technology. Meanwhile students will have opportunities in workshops to apply various ceramic techniques they learn in the lectures to the actual making of ceramics. This course can be taken as either an Art History or a Studio Arts elective course but students are required to declare their choice of attribution in their final year of attendance.
courses.learning_outcomes:

- understand the history and basic technology of representative Chinese ceramics;
- develop skills in writing essay on ceramics; and

- apply their knowledge on Chinese ceramic technology in making ceramics.

courses.description: With special emphasis on recent excavations, this course will look at the artistic and technological development of cast-bronze artifacts from around 1600 BCE to the last few centuries BCE, in an attempt to understand China’s unique achievements in relation to other ancient Bronze Age civilizations. Ritual bronze vessels and weapons will be studied as reflections not only of artistic developments, but also of political, social, religious, and economic developments of the time. courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Western Art II: from Impressionism to the Late 1940s
courses.description: The latter part of the nineteenth century was a time of economic, technological and social change when traditional values were challenged. This revolutionary period marked the beginning of the modern age and the birth of many avant-garde movements. This course aims to examine the various manifestations of these movements and the utopian project they tried to materialize.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- know the general timeframe of the developments of Western art from the late 19th century to the 1960s;
- be able to situate an artwork (sculpture, painting and architecture) within a time period: for example, recognize whether a painting is a Pointillist or a Futurist work; and

- understand the historical background of each period and art movement.

- be able to think and write like an art historian, a curator and an art critic.
courses.course_syllabus:
- Introduction: Academic art

- Before Impressionism, Macchiaioli, Impressionism

- Symbolism and Art Nouveau

- Post-Impressionism

- Expressionism

- The School of Paris and Cubism

- De Stijl and the Bauhaus

- The Dada movement

- Surrealism

- Abstract Expressionism

Offerings with different subtitles can be taken up for credit, up to a maximum of two times.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- master various techniques in brushwork, tone variation and coloring in Chinese expressive painting;
- copy typical styles by some important Chinese expressive painters; and

- have their own creation in Chinese expressive painting
courses.course_syllabus:
The topics will include:

- exercise of various techniques and formats of expression in Chinese expressive painting including outlining, boneless, ink, light colouring, heavy colouring, medium style and free style; and

- study of typical style by some important Chinese expressive painters.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Sculpting Moulding and Casting
courses.description: This course introduces various perspectives of making sculpture in organic form through direct and indirect methods. Focus will be on understanding and transforming forms in nature as well as forms derived from nature. Technical studio will include hand-building techniques, molding, and casting. Students will learn how to think through three dimensional forms and transforming ideas into tangible objects.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- enhance their skill of 3D design;
- understand the history, knowledge and practical theory of molding and casting; and

- create a bronze sculpture – from idea and concept development, material and structure analysis, thorough techniques application to build and construction.
courses.course_syllabus:
Duplication: Duplication from one mold in plaster.

Mold our Body: Figure molding in fiber glass.

From geometry to organism: Abstract sculpture in bronze casting.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. The CU Forum will also be used if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Art in Public Space
courses.description:
Art in pubic space became an important subject for those who wanted to reconcile the conflict between individual artistic ideas and the common norms in public space in 21st century. The presumption that artist cannot contribute significantly to public sector is questionable nowadays—there are many experiments being done to show that artistic intervention can somehow arouse the awareness of hidden power and political structure of the society. As a citizen or an artist, art has a role to play in public space.
Seeing public space as an arena for artistic expression becomes another focus in contemporary art. Gallery, museum which are supposed to be neutral space in showing art is gradually replaced by space with cultural significances; message in art has been put into specific context for holistic examination.
The course will cover various art forms in public space, which include traditionally defined public art, such as sculpture and functional objects in public space. The course will also teach student how to realize an art project through management and negotiation among different stakeholders— the client, the public, the technical consultant and the artist.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students should be able to:

1) understand various forms of public art and their function

2) understand the infra structure and function of different parties involved

3) understand flow and operation of public art project

4) understand communication and contractual matter
courses.course_syllabus:
1) art, context and space

2) politics, esthetics and function in public art

3) various forms in public art

4) operation and flow of public art project

5) technical matter—manufacture, legal matter and engagement of the general public

6) individual and group project
courses.feedback_evaluation: Evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. The CU Forum will also be used if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Experimental Drawing
courses.description: This course will introduce various approaches to drawing so as to broaden students’ technical abilities and provoke awareness of contemporary approaches. Emphasis will be made on five major areas: (1) understanding rhythm and lyrical elements of drawing through figure movement; (2) exploring pictorial composition by studying and recomposing classical works; (3) developing visual vocabulary by exercising free association; (4) drawing as narrative; and (5) drawing as a tool to map spaces and activities.

- create drawing tools

- apply drawing in different media.

- digital manipulation of drawing

- drawing & text

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Sculpture: Building and Carving
courses.description: This course will focus on the classic approaches to sculpture: addition and subtraction. Metal and wood will be the major materials used. Through addition, students will be trained to weld metal and join wood to produce large-scale sculpture. Through subtraction, students will be able to define details and inner spaces of sculptures.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- understand various fundamental techniques of wood carving and welding;
- further master the usage of power tools; and

- explore the possibilities of concept by studying and analysis of various Western and Chinese sculptors

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Figure Sketching
courses.description: The content of this course includes knowledge of human anatomy, sketching of head and statues and sketching of live model. Through a series of exercises, students will acquire a better understanding of depicting human forms. It also enhances students’ powers of expression in figure drawing. The course helps to build the foundation of figure painting which applies for different media.

This course is designed for students who are interested to learn figure drawing.

The course covers fundamental knowledge of the human anatomy for artistic purpose, introduction of the drawing of the masters, sketching exercises from life of heads, features, faces and the draped figure.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Calligraphy in Ancient Scripts
courses.description: This course aims to provide a more focused study of ancient Chinese calligraphy before the Qin and Han dynasties. In addition to the training of writing skills, the knowledge of paleography will also be introduced. The course lays a foundation for further pursuit of creation and study. This course can be taken as either an Art History or a Studio Arts elective course but students are required to declare their choice of attribution in their final year of attendance.
courses.learning_outcomes:
This course aims to provide a more focused study in ancient Chinese calligraphy before Qin and Han dynasties. In addition to the training of writing skills, the knowledge of paleography will also be introduced in the course. The design of the course is a foundation for further pursuit of creation and study.

It is through the study of Chinese calligraphy in ancient scripts that students can understand the relationship of art and the culture of ancient Chinese society, and can widen their horizon in calligraphy creation, setting a foundation for their study of ancient Chinese art in future.
courses.course_syllabus:
The topics will include:

- introduction to ancient Chinese Calligraphy;
- writing skills in ancient Chinese Calligraphy including oracle-bone inscription, bronze inscription,

stone engravings, bamboo and wooden strips inscription before Qin and Han dynasties; and

- knowledge of paleography.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. The CU Forum will also be used if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Gongbi: Chinese Delineative Painting
courses.description: This is an advanced study of Chinese Painting. It focuses on studying the techniques of painting by using fine-line and wash, one of two primary painting techniques in the Chinese tradition. Technique and forms of expression such as ink, light colouring, heavy colouring are included. Students will be encouraged to initiate artistic creation by incorporating different forms of expression. Contents of this course will vary with instructors and students. Offerings with different subtitles can be taken up for credit, up to a maximum of two times.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- have a thorough understanding of techniques and forms of expression of Chinese delineative painting;
- be able to master the techniques; and

- possess the ability of independent creation
courses.course_syllabus:
The topics will include:

- introduction to materials and tools used in Chinese delineative painting;
- exercise of techniques in brushwork, tone variation and colouring; and

- study of typical style by some important Chinese expressive painters.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. The CU Forum will also be used if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Chinese Landscape Painting
courses.description: This course includes introduction of different tools and landscape painting skills. The main learning methods are imitation and copy. It aims to allow students get familiar with traditional painting methods and combine with outdoor sketches to finally have a complete understanding in Chinese landscape painting. The students are expected to be able to integrate what he/she has learned, so as to develop a personal painting style finally.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

1. master various techniques in brushwork, tone variation and coloring in Chinese landscape painting;
2. copy typical styles by some important Chinese landscape painters; and

3. have their own creation in Chinese landscape painting
courses.course_syllabus:
- exercise of various techniques and formats of expression in Chinese landscape painting including Tree method, Mountain and stone method, Cloud and water method ,Landscape decorated method, Composition, Landscape in different time, Color arrangementaint , Paint from life ; and

- study of typical style by some important Chinese landscape painters.
courses.feedback_evaluation: A clear teaching plan will be provided before the course commences. An evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. An on line discussion platform may be installed if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Ceramic Art: Form and Space
courses.description: The course introduces construction skills and knowledge in ceramics through studio practice. Focus will be placed on the creation of form using clay and methods in ceramics. Discussions and critiques will be arranged to enhance the awareness of ceramics in the context of visual culture and contemporary life. Students are encouraged to apply the skills and knowledge learnt in developing their own area of work.

The topics will include:

1. Health and safety in ceramic studios

2. Knowledge of material process in clay

3. Hand-building methods and their variations

4. Investigating notions of space through ceramic works

5. Interaction between form and space

6. Perceiving and creating three dimensional forms in contemporary life

courses.title: Art and Social Engagement: Summer Internship

meetings.instructor:
Dr. WONG Wai Yin, 
Mr. LEUNG Ho Yin Harry

Dr. WONG Wai Yin, 
Mr. LEUNG Ho Yin Harry

courses.description:
How to engage society through artistic and creative strategies? This course is designed to respond to this question with hands-on experience. This course provides internship placements for students to work with organizations involved in social engagement through creative thinking. Designed in an inter-disciplinary approach, students will not only work closely with artists, but also with social workers, entrepreneurs, designers, therapists, architects, etc. Besides art-administrative works, students are expected to create art projects and assist the organizations in running creative projects during the placement period. Through practical works, students are expected to build capacity to conduct socially-engaged projects in their own in future.
Period of placement usually starts from June to August. All interested students must attend the briefing session conducted in February/March.
This course can be taken as either an Art History or a Studio Arts elective course but students are required to declare their choice of attribution in their final year of attendance.
courses.course_syllabus:
For Undergraduate students year 2 or above, year 3 prioritized. Internship usually starts from June to August. Approximately 150 working hours in 8-10 weeks in total.

Internship Briefing Session in February or March

Matching and interviews with partner institutions in March and April

Internship placement in the agreed period at respective partner institutions

End-of-placement presentation in October or November

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Art As Profession
courses.description: This course provides students with information and training in preparation for careers as art professionals. Emphasis will be placed on how the art producer, art mediator and the general public interact with each other. Topics will include cultural policies in Hong Kong, the role of the public and private sectors, art in public spaces, art criticism, curatorship, organizing portfolios, exhibitions, and legal issues. In addition, guest speakers will speak on their experiences as art professionals in Hong Kong. This course can be taken as either an Art History or a Studio Arts elective course but students are required to declare their choice of attribution in their final year of attendance.
courses.course_syllabus:
Artist entities and their environment

Artist entities and the public sector

Artist entities and the private sector

How to prepare one to become an art-related professional
courses.feedback_evaluation: Evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. The CU Forum will also be used if appropriate.

3. Cultural Economy edited by Paul du Gay and Michael Pryke, Sage, 2003

4. Baseline Study on Hong Kong's Creative Industries 2003 (Central Policy Unit, The Govt of HKSAR)

5. A Study on Creativity Index, 2005 (Home Affairs Bureau, The Govt of HKSAR)

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Seal Carving
courses.description: The advanced course in Chinese arts creation includes an introduction to the materials, tools and techniques of seal carving, and the imprinting and rubbing of seals. Through the analysis and study of representative examples over the centuries, as well as the art of modern seal carving, students can master the different script-forms in seal carving and a wide-range of carving techniques, toward creating their own styles in seal carving. Students are advised to take FAAS3223 before taking this course.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students will be able to:

- have a thorough understanding of the history and theory of seal carving;
- possess the ability of independent creation; and

- develop personal styles
courses.course_syllabus:
The topics will include:

- introduction to materials and tools;
- exercise of techniques in carving and seal stamping, as well as carving and rubbing the side colophon; and

- studies of various masterpieces styles of different dynasties.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Modern Ink Painting
courses.description: This course aims to tackle similarities and dissimilarities between Eastern and Western painting, specifically to guide students to experiment with Western ideas and methods in the Chinese ink media, or to attempt contemporary expressions with the subject matter and techniques of traditional Chinese painting. Instead of large classes, students will be tutored with discussions and demonstrations, individually or in small groups, to establish independent paths of artistic pursuit.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will be able to:

- employ the Chinese ink media, with or without combining appropriate Western painting media, in their creative pursuit

- possess the ability of independent creation
courses.course_syllabus:
Media: Liquid ink and water, wet and dry media, different solvents

Materials: oriental paper, other kinds of paper, other surfaces

Techniques: Chinese brushes, other mark-making tools, rubbing, transferring, and collage

Elements: Dots and lines, planes and volumnes, marks and objects

Composition: Parallel and intersecting structures, melodic flows, rhythmic variations

Concept: Representation of reality, reorganization of reality, ideas and feelings

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: History of Chinese Literati Painting
courses.description: The course aims at tracing the development of literati painting with special reference to literati views on painting held by Su Shi and others since the Song dynasty, the styles and influences of leading painters, and the issue of transformation on the basis of the great painting traditions. Topics to be covered include theories of literati painting, personal styles and regional painting schools, perpetuation and reinvention of the traditions, paintings as commodities, and elitist and popular tastes. Students of the course are required to conduct thematic research and submit an essay at the end of the term.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Contemporary Art
courses.description: The goal of this course is to explore Western art from approximately 1945 to the mid- 1990s and especially the shift from a modernist to a postmodern culture, as well as how artists have endeavored to address a new era characterized by globalization and consumerism. In parallel, this course will also trace the development of Chinese art during the 20th century. Special attention will be paid to Chinese artists’ response to the political turmoil and cultural changes of the last fifty years.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Fine Arts
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students will be able to:
- understand the general timeframe of the developments of Chinese and Western art from the 1960s to the present days;
- understand the social and artistic background of the works of a large number of contemporary artists in order to make them aware of the latest developments in the art field as well as help them find meaning in their own art practices; and
- create an informed opinion on any artwork produced in the postmodern period.

Skill:

This course aims at preparing students with necessary skill and tools to understand their own strength and weakness, thus to cope with the challenge of graduation project. Special attention will be given to developing individual beliefs in art, research method and technique, and presentation skill. As and when appropriate, students will also be divided into a few groups and supervised by tutors / specialists of different art practices.

Students are required to submit a written artist statement related to their graduation project and participate in the thesis show (graduation show) as course and graduation requirement.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Fine Arts
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students will be able to, particularly in the areas of ‘knowledge, skill, and attitude”:

Knowledge:
- identify research direction, collect reference and data, and provide guidance on conceptual development; and
- monitor their progress of research and polish their concepts;

Skill:
- understand their strength and weakness, thus to develop necessary skill to cope with their objectives in their graduation work; and
- polish their presentation skills, in order to achieve a better result despite various constrains.

Attitude:
- develop professionalism in art through collaborative work with classmate to realize the graduation exhibition;
- be responsible to serious spectators, develop a clear internal logic of work, and provide sufficient hints for further inquiry of the work; and
- develop work ethics.
courses.course_syllabus:

- review different models and methodogies in making.of contemporary art.
- prepare theoretical text as document to support artistic creation.
- display strategy, art exbibition as venue for re-creation of a studio work.
- individual supervision by external supervisor.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Chinese Art History
courses.description: This course aims to provide foundational knowledge of Chinese art history from the Neolithic period to the Qing Dynasty. It introduces to students major artistic traditions of China, such as pottery, jade, bronze, porcelain, and painting. Their material properties, production techniques, developments, masterpieces and their producers, key research topics and methods will be discussed. Through essay reading, class presentation and discussion, and academic paper writing, this course will enhance students’ academic research abilities and lay a comprehensive foundation for further in-depth research on various materials and topics in Chinese art history.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Pottery (I)**
Materials and production techniques: clay, glaze and kiln firing.
Overview of Neolithic pottery traditions from 6000BCE to 2000BCE.
Form and decoration of potteries in relation to different Neolithic settlements.

* *2. Pottery (II)**
Material and technological development: white pottery, proto-porcelain and lead glaze.
Overview of pottery development from 2000BCE to 907CE.
Pottery vessels, funeral figurines and models, and the contexts of their use.

* *3. Jade (I)**
Material properties and distribution of Jade and Jadeite. Production techniques: ground and polish.
Overview of jade development from 4000BCE to 2000BCE.
Masterpieces and their archaeological contexts.

* *4. Jade (II)**
Overview of jade development from 2000BCE to 1BCE.
Ritual jade, burial jade, jade as symbols of moral virtue and agents for immortality.

* *5. Bronze (I)**
Metallurgical and metalworking techniques.
Overview of the bronze development from 2000BCE to 771BCE.
Bronze vessels, weapons, ritual vessels and music instruments.
Masterpieces and their archaeological contexts.

* *6. Bronze (II)**
New style and casting methods.
Overview of the bronze development from 771BCE to 221BCE.
Historical significance and cultural impact of bronze inscriptions.

* *7. Porcelain (I)**
Material differences between pottery, stoneware and porcelain.
Advancement in kiln design, kiln furniture and firing technology.
Overview of Stoneware and porcelain development from 220CE to 1279CE.
Monochrome and polychrome glazed wares.
The rise of production sites all over China.

* *8. Porcelain (II)**
The rise of Jingdezhen.
Overview of porcelain development from 1279CE to 1911CE.
The establishment of Imperial Factory and its impact.
New glaze, decoration and production technology from Europe to China.

* *9. Painting (I)**
Materials and pigments: Lacquer, silk and paper.
Early painting on lacquerwares and silk banners.
Court paintings and the taste for realistic images and gongbi meticulous style.

* *10. Painting (II)**
Court painting and literati painting.
Overview of Chinese painting development from the Song to the Ming dynasties.
Masterpieces and their respective painters and social contexts.

* *11. Painting (III)**
Paintings by professional painters of the Ming and Qing dynasties.
Popular themes and styles for the painting markets.
Interaction between painters and patrons.
courses.feedback_evaluation: A clear teaching plan will be provided before the course commences. An evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. An online discussion platform may be installed if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Modern and Contemporary Asian Art
courses.description:
The course examines the defining moments in the development of modern and contemporary art, primarily in East Asia, with selected topics from South-Asia or South-East Asia as points of comparison. Anchored by a significant movement or trend, each session focuses on the relationship between selected artistic events and the social, political, cultural, and technological conditions of its emergence. A wide range of media, from painting, sculpture, and print-making to photography, video, installation, and performance art, will be considered. Situating the key aesthetic transformations that defined art’s modernity and contemporary transformations in a broader historical context, the course explores art historical discourses and approaches that have been developed very recently, with an aim to forge an analysis of an emerging subject. Their relationship to the rise of global art discourses about non-Western art as well as its cross-sections with modern and contemporary art in Europe and North America will be heeded. Students will examine the tempus and locus of the modern and contemporary, as well as the social and cultural conditions of their developments, and the important works and “texts” that constitute the global contemporary.

The case studies in this course offer a testing ground to think about new approaches to writing art histories and displaying contemporary art. The following questions will be explored: How is “modern,” “contemporary” and “avant-garde” in China, Japan, and India different from their European counterparts? What are the moments that constitute “modernity” and “contemporaneity” in Asian art? Are “styles,” “isms” and “movements” appropriate criteria to judge modern and contemporary Asian Art? What would be other ways to contextualize these global trends?

courses.course_syllabus:
* *Introduction: From Modernism to Modernisms and Contemporaneity**
This introductory session provides an overview on the emergence and dynamics of Modern art movements in Asia. We will discuss selected art historical papers on East Asian Modernism and contemporaneity, particularly in China and Japan.

* *Colonialism and Cultural Transmissions**
This session is devoted to the history of cultural interchange between Asia and Europe since the late 19th Century. We will explore the system of arts education and institution in early modernism, as well as the issue of transmission of style.

* *Modern Art in Japan**
This session deals with Modern art in Japan and is divided into three subtopics: Yoga, Nihonga, and Calligraphic abstraction. The construction of these concepts, the hybridity of styles, as well as their transcultural historical backgrounds will be tackled.

* *Critique of Euroamerican Modernism: Japan and Korea**
Two sessions will be devoted to three art “schools” or “movements” in East Asia, namely Monoha, Tansaekhwa, and Gutai which consciously responded to Eurocamerican modern art. We will compare and analyse their diverse strategies of gestural abstraction and environmentalism.

* *Modern Art in India**
One session will provide an overview of Indian modernism until the 1970s, from academic Realism, Abstraction, to pop art.

* *Modern Art in the Chinese Speaking World**
Two sessions will be devoted to various “schools” of Realism and Abstraction in China from the early 20th century to the 1960s.

* *Identity, Cultural Representation, and the Biennale Effect post-1980s**
Two sessions will be devoted to the turn to the global contemporary. Topics we will discuss include New Wave Art in China, Indian Art since the 1980s, the biennale effect, as well art exchange within Asia-Pacific.

* *Activism and Performance in Contemporary Asian Art**
Two sessions will be devoted to avant-garde, body art, and intermedia in Japan, China, and Indonesia.

* *Gender Perspectives and Women Artists**
This session provides an overview on images of women, women artists and feminist criticisms since modernism.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Painting in East Asia
courses.description: China, Japan, and Korea each have a long and rich painting history, yet the works from these three countries share common elements in format, theme, technique, and theory. Since the Tang and Song dynasties, the ruling classes, scholars, monks, collectors, and painters across East Asia have been familiar with and have highly valued paintings from neighboring regions. Therefore, this course will not only introduce the representative paintings and their artistic styles from the three countries and the social, cultural, religious, political, and economic contexts that shaped their unique expressions and distributions, but will also explore how cross-border interactions, both public and private, contributed to the formation of a distinct East Asian art world.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
1. The Origin and Development of Chinese Painting: To explore what defines Chinese painting as distinctly Chinese.
2. The Origin and Development of Japanese Painting: To explore what defines Japanese painting as distinctly Japanese.
3. The Origin and Development of Korean Painting: To explore what defines Korean painting as distinctly Korean.
4. Eight Views Painting in East Asia: To introduce the literary and political backgrounds of the Eight Views paintings popular in China, Japan, and Korea, as well as the different artistic expressions of this theme in each country.
5. Chinese Paintings in Japanese Collections (I) Ningbo Buddhist Painting: To introduce the commission and artistic features of Ningbo Buddhist paintings, and their transmission to Japan.
6. Chinese Paintings in Japanese Collections (II) Kundaikan sayū chōki: To introduce the different connoisseurship of painting between China and Japan through examining Chinese paintings from the shogun’s collection and Japanese documents.
7. Goryeo Buddhist Painting: To introduce the visual features of extant Goryeo Buddhist paintings and their transmission in Korea, Japan, and beyond.
8. Ink Painting in East Asia: To explore the origins and differences between Chinese and Japanese ink painting.
9. Zhe School Painting in East Asia: To explain the definition of the “Zhe School” and this painting style’s popularity and decline in China, Japan, and Korea.
10. Literati Painting in East Asia: To assess the origin of the painting theory of the “Southern and Northern Schools” and the varying expressions and adoption by painters from various social classes of the Chinese literati painting style in different countries.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Chinese Ceramics in Global Contexts
courses.description: This course traces the development of Chinese ceramics in local and global contexts from the 16th to the 19th century. It explores Ming and Qing China and its exchanges and mutual learning with the world’s civilizations in the field of ceramics. Major topics include the economic and social background of the Ming and Qing dynasties and the development of the ceramics industry, the opening of direct sea routes between China and the world and the global circulation of Chinese ceramics, the exchange of culture, art, science, and technology using ceramics as a medium, the spread of Chinese ceramics to the West and the key role it played in the rise of the European porcelain industry. Through studies of objects, combined with Chinese and Western documentary records and historical images, the course will reconstruct the development of Chinese ceramics and its unique craft and cultural achievements in the globalization matrix, and provide insights into the unique role of Chinese ceramics in the development of world ceramics.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Introduction to the Course**
- Provide a general idea of the background, structure, and focus of the course.

* *Encounter of Oriental Wonders I: The Opening of Direct Sea Routes between East and West**
- The Age of Discovery and the first encounters between East and West via sea routes.
- New aspiration for wealth of Asia and the mysterious land of China among European royals and aristocrats.

* *Encounter of Oriental Wonders II: The Traditional and New Markets for Chinese Ceramics**
- The circulation of Chinese ceramics on the Asian markets before the 16th century: Korea, Japan, Southeast Asia and the Middle East as examples.
- Porcelain fever: Chinese porcelain bought/ordered in Asia and brought to Europe by the Portuguese explorers.

* *The Thriving Country of China I: Ming and Qing China in the 16th to 17th Centuries**
- Economic diversification and specialization as a result of political stability, population growth and the general improvement of living conditions in the mid to late Ming period.
- The economic environment, tax reform and improvement of transport networks in Ming and Qing China.
- The flourishing of arts and crafts [including porcelain firing] throughout China from the mid-Ming period onwards.

* *The Thriving Country of China II: Export Ceramics Produced in China**
- The most representative types of Chinese export ceramics from the 16th to 19th centuries.
- The main production centres for export ceramics: Jingdezhen in Jiangxi Province; Yixing in Jiangsu Province; and Zhangzhou and Dehua in Fujian Province.

* *Manufacture and Transport of Porcelain: Historical Documents and Export Paintings**
- Documents showing that the European missionaries, explorers and merchants who lived in China had been trying to make secret enquiries or records about porcelain since the late Ming period.
- As a special type of export commodity, some of the export paintings of Canton illustrate the manufacture, transport and sale of export porcelain.

* *Braving the Ocean Waves I: The Global Transport of Chinese Ceramics**
- The main routes of merchant ships from the 16th to the 19th century.
- The major countries and companies that controlled the trading ports and sailing routes.

* *Braving the Ocean Waves II: Shipwrecks and their Ceramic Cargoes**
- Famous shipwrecks from the 16th to 19th centuries.
- Underwater archaeology and the ceramic treasures discovered and salvaged from deep sea.

* *Fashion of All Nations I: Chinese Ceramics in Asia and the New World of the Americas**
- Chinese ceramics in Japan: tea ceremony, flower ceremony, and Kaiseki-ryōri.
- Chinese ceramics in Southeast Asia and the Middle East: daily objects, rural artefacts and indicators of family wealth.
- Chinese ceramics in the New World of the Americas: porcelain currency.

* *Fashion of All Nations II: Chinese Ceramics in Europe**
- Chinese porcelain wares and the daily life and artistic tastes of European royals and aristocrats; chinoiserie; fashion for porcelain table services, and porcelain sets for tea, coffee, and chocolate drinking.
- Chinaware copying the shapes of Western glassware and metalware; decorative themes derived from Western myths, biblical stories and coats of arms; brilliant overglaze enamels of falangcai (lit. enamel or foreign), famille rose and Canton porcelains inspired by the technique of painted enamel in Europe.

* *Profound Impact of Chinaware I: Imitations from Japan and Europe**
- Japanese kilns in Arita and their imitation of Chinaware; the export and popularity of Imari porcelain.
- Porcelain wares made in Jingdezhen and decorated in European workshops.
- The explorations of European artists, scientists and potters in search of the secrets of Chinese porcelain.

* *Profound Impact of Chinaware II: The Rise of the European Porcelain Manufactory**
- Augustus II the Strong and his passion for Chinese porcelain; the first European hard-paste porcelain created in Meissen.
- Royal Delft and the efforts of the Dutch to copy Chinese blue-and-white.
- The unique role that Chinese porcelain played in the rise of the European porcelain manufactory from the 18th century onwards.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Chinese Art Display and Communication
courses.description: This course explores the ways in which knowledge and concepts of Chinese art and antiquities are conveyed to contemporary audiences through public display. A range of contexts in which Chinese art and antiquities are curated will be discussed, from the display of actual art objects in public and private museums, commercial galleries, and auction houses, to the use of digital media to showcase Chinese art and antiquities in virtual and physical museums. Students will be equipped with basic theories of art curation and communication, exposed to different settings of art display, engaged in dialogues with curators, and encouraged to reflect on various strategies of Chinese art curation through case studies. Site visits will be arranged to provide students with firsthand experience of different types of art display and their effectiveness in communicating with the audience. Museum curators and art specialists will be invited to share their curatorial concepts and experiences.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Introduction to Chinese Art Collection and Display**
How did Chinese art and antiquities become collectibles worldwide from the 19th century, leading to the establishment of major museums and collections where the majority of public displays of Chinese art and antiquities take place nowadays?

* *2. Display of Art and Antiquities in Public and Private Museums**
What are the core characteristics and features of public and private museums? Why is curation important in the display of art and antiquities?

* *3. Case Study I: Chinese Art Exhibition in Museums**
What are the factors that contribute to a “successful” exhibition of Chinese art? How does the display of art objects shape our understanding of the objects and its related history?
An ongoing exhibition will be chosen as the subject of discussion with site visit followed by class discussion.

* *4. Case Study II: Chinese Art Exhibition in Museums**
How does the combination of art object display, virtual exhibition tours, and publication of exhibition catalogues influence the viewer's perception of the featured art objects and the curatorial concepts of the exhibition?
An exhibition will be chosen as the subject of discussion.

* *5. In Dialogue with Curators**
How do museum curators convey curatorial concepts through art objects? What are the factors that influence the curatorial direction, selection, and interpretation of exhibits, as well as the presentation strategies of an exhibition? How should an exhibition be assessed, and how can visitor reactions be gauged?
A museum curator will be invited to share with students.

* *6. Display of Art and Antiquities in Commercial Spaces**
What are the characteristics and features of commercial spaces as venues for art display?

* *7. Site Visit**
What strategies are employed to create meaning and value for Chinese art and antiquities displayed in various commercial venues?
Guided tour will be arranged to visit commercial galleries or auction houses.

* *8. In Dialogue with Auction House Specialists**
How is value created through the display and cataloguing of art objects?
An auction house or gallery specialist will be invited to share with students.

* *9. Digital Technology in Art Display**
What kinds of digital technology are involved in the display of Chinese art and antiquities in museums?

* *10. Curation through Digital Media**
How does cultural understanding and interpretation reach audiences through the display of art objects and monuments via digital media? How do audiences react to this alternative form of art display?

* *11. Case Study**
How far can curatorial concepts and knowledge of Chinese art be conveyed through digital media to audiences in comparison with the display of physical art objects?
Past or present examples of using the technology of Virtual Reality (VR) or Augmented Reality (AR) to disseminate knowledge and concepts of Chinese art and antiquities will be selected for discussion.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Chinese Calligraphy: Theory, Masterpieces and Connoisseurship
courses.description: This course traces the development of Chinese calligraphy from pre-Qin periods to the twentieth century. In addition to calligraphic theories, scripts, major calligraphers and their masterworks and styles, this course will also examine the philosophical ideas embodied in calligraphy, thus providing students with a better understanding of the historical development, cultural meaning, legacy, and connoisseurship in Chinese calligraphy.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Calligraphy of the pre-Qin periods and the Han dynasty**
- An introduction of Chinese calligraphy, its script development and use in daily life.
- Transformation of oracle bone inscription, bronze inscription, seal script and clerical script.

* *2. Calligraphy of the Wei, Jin and Northern and Southern dynasties**
- Transformation of cursive, running, and regular scripts.
- The Two Wangs traditions, as well as stele carving in the period.

* *3. Calligraphy of the Sui, Tang and Five dynasties**
- Regular and cursive scripts.
- Calligraphy masters and their theories of the Tang dynasty.

* *4. Calligraphy of the Northern Song dynasty**
- Su Shi, Wang Tingjian, Mi Fu and the Song expressive calligraphic styles.
- Model calligraphies from the Chunhua era (Chunhua ge tie).

* *5. Calligraphy of the Southern Song dynasty**
- The classical calligraphic style in the imperial court.
- Rubbings of stone engraving and model calligraphies.

* *6. Calligraphy of the Yuan dynasty**
- Zhao Mengfu and the revivalism of Yuan calligraphy.

* *7. Calligraphy of the Ming dynasty**
- Wen Zhengming and the Wu School of calligraphy.
- Dong Qichang and the Huating School of calligraphy.

* *8. Calligraphy of the Qing dynasty I**
- Model calligraphies and the Two Wangs tradition in the early and middle Qing.

* *9. Calligraphy of the Qing dynasty II**
- The stele school of calligraphy in the late Qing, its theory and innovation.

* *10. Calligraphy of the twentieth century**
- Different inspirations, schools and characteristics.
- Challenges of calligraphy in a new era.

* *11. Hong Kong calligraphy**
- The continuity of calligraphic tradition in Hong Kong.
- Calligraphy, and cultural preservation and daily life in Hong Kong.

* *12. Case study I**
- The debate of Preface to the Orchid Pavilion Gathering (Lanting xu)

* *13. Case study II**
- The debate of Monk Huai Su’s Autobiography (Zixu tie).
courses.feedback_evaluation: A clear teaching plan will be provided before the course commences. An evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. An online discussion platform may be installed if appropriate.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Buddhist Art and Material Culture
courses.description: This course is designed to enhance student’s ability to conduct research on Buddhist visual and material culture from around 5th century BCE to 20th century CE. It approaches the subject from a trans-Asiatic perspective and introduces to students different research methods. Major topics include (1) worship icons, (2) iconography and iconometry, (3) manuscripts and pictorial narratives, (4) śarīra and other charismatic objects, (5) ritual space and sacred architecture, (6) artistic styles and conventions, and (7) patronage and socio-political context. Students are expected to acquire in-depth understanding of the development, function and meaning of Buddhist art through studying archaeological and extant art and artefacts in their respective religious settings. They are also required to undertake independent research on selected topics at the end of the course.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Introduction; India I: Maurya, Śuṅga**
How is sacrality construed in society?
The Buddha, bodhisattvas, aniconism, stupa, jātaka, caitya, vihāra

* *2. India II: Kuṣān, Sātavāhana**
How does society visualize a divinity?
Gandhāra, Mathurā, Andhra

* *3. India III: Gupta, Post-Gupta, and Pāla**
How differently is beauty conceived in different societies?
Sārnāth, Vākāṭaka

* *4. Central Asia**
How do different communities interact with one another?
Bamiyan, Khotan, Kucha, Kumtura

* *5. Sri Lanka & Southeast Asia**
How does one thing in a society mean something else in another?
Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa, Borobudur, Bagan

* *6. Himalayas & Mongolia**
How does a selected few control knowledge in society?
Tantra, thangka, mandala

* *7. China I: Eastern Han (25-220 CE) to Jin (265-420)**
What does it take for a society come to terms with a new ideology?
Soul jars (hunping魂瓶), Kharoṣṭhī, piece-mold casting

* *8. China II: Northern Wei (386-535)**
How does society alter things according to necessity?
Dunhuang, Binglingsi, Yungang, Sinicization

* *9. China III: Eastern/Western Wei, Northern Qi/Northern Zhou, Sui**
How does religion work in service of politics?
Persecution, Latter Days, archaeological hoards

* *10. China IV: Tang, Five Dynasties, Song, Liao & Jin**
How does society preserve values?
Relics and reliquaries, pagoda, timber-frame building

* *11. Korea**
How does society deal with a foreign culture?
Three Kingdoms, Unified Silla, Koryo

* *12. Japan**
How is it like when there are competing beliefs?
Asuka, Nara, Heian, Kamakura

• Denise Patry Leidy, The Art of Buddhism: An Introduction to Its History and Meaning (Boston: Shambhala, 2009).

* *India I: Maurya, Śuṅga**
Leidy, 1-5; Seckel, 11-21, 25-46; Kooij, 7-19.
Cf. Susan L. Huntington, "Aniconism and the Multivalence of Emblems: Another Look," Ars Orientalis 22 (1992), 111-156.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Ancient Chinese Bronzes and Metal Art
courses.description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of ancient Chinese bronzes, focusing on their artistic, technological, and cultural significance. Students will examine the featured research and scholarly debates surrounding the production, function, and iconography of these important artifacts from China’s remote past. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and examination of museum objects, students will develop a nuanced understanding of the role of bronze in shaping China’s material culture and artistic traditions.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Introduction to Ancient Chinese Bronzes**
Overview of the Bronze Age in China
Significance of bronzes in early Chinese civilization
Survey of major bronze artifact types and their functions

* *2. Bronze Technology and Production**
Metallurgical and metalworking techniques
Specialized workshops and the organization of bronze production
Regional variations in bronze-making traditions

* *3. Ritual Bronzes**
Vessel and weaponry forms in ancestral worship
Ritual bells and early Chinese ceremony
Case studies of major bronze ritual assemblages

* *4. Bronzes in Funerary and Elite Court Contexts**
The display of wealth and status by funerary bronzes
The role of bronzes in court life and elite activities
Political significance of early Chinese bronzes

* *5. Bronzes and Early Chinese State Development**
Bronze production, labor organization and state formation
Bronze use in the consolidation of regional polities
Bronzes as expressions of regional and imperial identities

* *6. Bronzes, Legitimacy, and the Mandate of Heaven**
The Mandate of Heaven: Historical Context and Interpretation
The jiuding tripods: legend, history, and bronzes as symbols of legitimacy
Modern reinterpretations of ancient practices

* *7. Archaism and Antiquarianism in Chinese Bronzes**
Defining Archaism and Antiquarianism in Chinese bronze art
Cultural significance of Chinese archaism
The role of antiquarianism in collecting and interpretation

* *8. Traditional Chinese Epigraphy: “Study on Metal and Stone”**
Early Chinese writing system in comparative perspective
Historical significance and cultural impact of bronze inscriptions
Techniques and styles in bronze inscriptions
Epigraphical study on archaic bronzes from Song to Qing Dynasties (960–1911 CE)

* *9. Modern Connoisseurship and Archaeological Study of Ancient Bronzes**
Stylistic analysis, dating, and authentication of bronzes
Issues of provenance and the circulation of ancient Chinese bronzes
The collecting and display of bronzes in museums
Archaeological study of ancient bronzes

* *10. Special Topics in Pre-Qin (before 221 BCE) Chinese Bronzes**
The section-mold casting: techniques and innovations
Critical perspectives on symbolism and iconography
Driving force from the Eurasian Steppe

* *11. Special Topics in Qin and Later Chinese Bronzes (Since 221 BCE)**
Technological Borrowing from the Hellenistic world?
Bronze art and cross-media interplay
Ethnography on multiple metalworking traditions

* *12. Current Debates and Research Directions**
Imaginary animals in bronze art: surface ornament or representation?
Technical debates on section-mold casting and lost-wax casting
Interdisciplinary approaches to studying ancient Chinese bronzes
