courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: Art, Time, and Technology
courses.description: This course introduces significant theories and case studies in media art history, spanning from the Enlightenment to the 21st century. The first part of the course will present an overview of the technological advancement in optical and sound media as well as their revival in the contemporary art scene. It will also provide theoretical frameworks for studying the aesthetics of moving image art, particularly the issues of temporality and performativity. The second part will focus on the development of the time-based media art in Asia. Topics and mediums discussed include the camera obscura, early film, intermedia installations, Net art, the digital turn, sound art, media art preservation, performance, and digital theatre. Class activities will include a visit to a local museum or archive, group discussions, and a presentation at the end of the semester. Drawing on key artworks and fundamental theories, it aims to guide students to explore how media-based practices evolved in various social environments responding to the history of technology, and develop research skills for academic writing and curating.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Introduction to the Time-based Media: History, Mediums, Concepts**
This session explores the early history of time-based media and telecommunication art. There will also be an introduction on the formation of media art history as a discipline.

* *The Materiality and Temporality of Film**
Two sessions will be devoted to the history of moving image, the documentation and representation of time in early time-based media, and the development of gallery film.

* *Photography**
This session will cope with the history and theory of photography, as well as how the digital turn impacted the photographic practice.

* *Video and Interactive Media**
This session explores the development from analogue and digital video to multi-media installation. It will also discuss transnational networks of intermedia practice such as Fluxus.

* *From Performativity to Digital Theatre**
Two sessions will be devoted to the history of performance art and the liveness discourse, as well as an overview on the genealogy of digital theatre.

* *Game Art and Net Art**
This session is devoted to the intersection of game art and video art. We will also discuss case studies of net art.

* *Data Capitalism**
This session explores the concept of born-digital objects and conservation practices. It will also draw from Hito Steyerl’s creative works and critical writings to discuss data capitalism and problems resulted by data discrimination in the metaverse.

* *Posthuman Positions**
This session is devoted to environmentalism in creative arts and live art.

* *Media Art in Asia**
Two sessions will be devoted to the history of video art in China and Japan. A field trip to a local museum will be integrated.

courses.subject: FAAS
courses.title: English Writing Workshop
courses.description: This course is designed to help students develop their skills in writing about the history of Chinese art in English. Structured as a series of writing workshops, it introduces key forms of writing related to Chinese art, such as catalogue entries and academic articles. Through the introduction of art terminology, analysis of exemplary writing samples—including their organization and style—and guided writing exercises, students will acquire essential skills for articulating Chinese art history in different formats. The course also familiarizes students with specialized vocabulary related to different types of Chinese art objects, laying a strong foundation for future, in-depth research and writing on Chinese art history in English.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chinese Art History
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction
Introduce different forms of writings about art history and their major differences.
Understand research ethics and referencing for art history.

2. Workshop (A-I) Short Essay
Review short essays, looking for the essential components and analyzing the organization.
Introduce key vocabularies for writing a short essay.

3. Workshop (A-II)
Draft, write and revise a short essay.

4. Workshop (B-I) Catalogue Entry: Art Objects
Review exhibition and collection catalogue entries on art objects such as ceramics, jade, bronze and lacquer.
Introduce key terms and concepts.
Analyze the essential components and the organization of an entry.

5. Workshop (B-II)
Draft, write and revise a catalogue entry about art objects.

6. Workshop (B-III)
Draft, write and revise a catalogue entry about art objects.

7. Workshop (C-I) Catalogue Entry: Painting and Calligraphy
Review exhibition and collection catalogue entries on painting and calligraphy.
Introduce key terms and concepts.
Analyze the essential components and the organization of an entry.

8. Workshop (C-II)
Draft, write and revise a catalogue entry about painting and calligraphy.

9. Workshop (C-III)
Draft, write and revise a catalogue entry about painting and calligraphy.

10. Workshop (D-I) Argumentative Article
Review academic articles, looking for the thesis and analyzing the organization of arguments.
Introduce key vocabularies for writing an argumentative article.

11. Workshop (D-II)
Review academic articles, looking for the thesis and analyzing the organization of arguments.
Introduce key vocabularies for writing an argumentative article.

12. Workshop (D-III)
Draft the outline of a short argumentative article with the entries composed in the previous lessons.

13. Workshop (D-IV)
Draft and write a short argumentative article.
courses.feedback_evaluation: A clear teaching plan will be provided before the course commences. An evaluation exercise will be held at end of the course. An online discussion platform may be installed if appropriate.
courses.required_readings:
1. Introduction
Sylvan Barnet, “What Formal Analysis is” and “Style as the Sharper of Form” in A Short Guide to Writing about Art, global edition (Hong Kong: Pearson Longman, 2015), 64-83.
Margaret R. Lazzari and Dona Schlesier, Exploring Art: A Global, Thematic Approach, second edition (Belmont, Calif.: Thomson/Wadsworth, 2005), 3-9, 15-18, 26-41, 81-88.

2. Workshop (A-I) Short Essay
Sylvan Barnet, “Seeing and Saying, “Subject Matter and Content,” and “Form and Content,” in A Short Guide to Writing about Art, global edition (Hong Kong: Pearson Longman, 2015), 85-93.
Denise P. Leidy, “Introduction,” How to Read Chinese Ceramics (New York: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, 2015), 9-17.

3. Workshop (A-II)
Margaret Medley, “The Potter and his Materials,” in The Chinese Potter: A Practical History of Chinese Ceramics (Oxford: Phaidon Press Limited, 1989), 11-15.
Stacey Pierson, “Production: Manufacturing and Industry,” in Chinese Ceramics (London: V&A Publishing, 2009), 11-41.

4. Workshop (B-I) Catalogue Entry: Art Objects
Sylvan Barnet, “Writing an Entry in an Exhibition Catalogue,” in A Short Guide to Writing about Art, global edition (Hong Kong: Pearson Longman, 2015), 162-169.
Maggie C. K. Wan and Jiang Fangting eds., Transcending Transience: Art and Culture of Late-Ming Jiangnan (Hong Kong: Department of Fine Arts and Art Museum, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, 2021).

5. Workshop (B-II)
Tao Wang, “Matter of Things: How to Read Ancient Chinese Bronzes” in Mirroring China's Past: Emperors, Scholars, and Their Bronzes, edited Amy R. Peltz and Elissa Park (Chicago, Illinois: The Art Institute of Chicago, 2018), 17-31.
Kwang-chih Chang, “The Chinese Bronze Age: A Modern Synthesis,” in The Great Bronze Age of China: An Exhibition from the People’s Republic of China, ed. Wen Fong (New York: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, 1980), 35-50.
Lothar Ledderose, “Casting of Bronzes the Complicated Way,” in Ten Thousand Things: Module and Mass Production in Chinese Art (Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2001), 25-49.

6. Workshop (B-III)
Jenny F. So, “Jade in Nature,” in Early Chinese Jades in the Harvard Art Museums (Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard Art Museums, 2019), 17-25.
James C. Y. Watt, “Jades,” in Possessing the Past: Treasures from the National Palace Museum, Taipei (New York: Metropolitan Museum of Art, 1996.), 41-71.

7. Workshop (C-I) Catalogue Entry: Painting and Calligraphy
Wang Yao-t’ing, the section “Ideas and Landscape” of “Chapter Two: Paradise on Earth” in Part I, Looking at Chinese Painting, (Tokyo: Nigensha Publishing Co., 1st ed.,1995 or 2nd ed., 2000), 23-6.
Wu Hung, “The Origins of Chinese Painting (Paleolithic to Tang Dynasty),” in Three Thousand Year of Chinese Painting, by Yang Xin, et al. (New Haven: Yale University and Foreign Language Press, 1997), 48-58, 63-71, 79-85.

8. Workshop (C-II)
Harold Mok ed, The Bei Shan Tang Legacy: Chinese Painting (Hong Kong: Department of Fine Arts and Art Museum, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, 2019).

9. Workshop (C-III)
Harold Mok ed, The Bei Shan Tang Legacy: Chinese Calligraphy (Hong Kong: Department of Fine Arts and Art Museum, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, 2014).

10. Workshop (D-I) Argumentative Article
Sylvan Barnet, “Why Write about Art?” “The Function of Critical Writing” “Some Words about Critical thinking” and “Expressing Opinions: The Writer’s ‘I’” in A Short Guide to Writing about Art, global edition (Hong Kong: Pearson Longman, 2015), 24-52.

11. Workshop (D-II)
Hui-shu Lee, “Bada Shanren’s Bird-and-Fish Painting and the Art of Transformation,” Archives of Asian Art, 1991, Vol. 44, 6-26.
Peter Sturman, “Su Shi Renders no Emotion,” The Journal of Chinese Literature and Culture, 6:1, 2019, 15-55.

12. Workshop (D-III) & 13. Workshop (D-IV)
